intro: The jealousy offering (Hebrew minḥat qĕnā'ōt) was the grain offering a husband brought to the tabernacle when he suspected his wife of adultery but had no witnesses (Num. 5:11–31). Made of barley meal without oil or frankincense (unlike regular grain offerings), it was called a "memorial" offering that would "bring iniquity to remembrance." The accused woman was brought before the priest, who made her drink the "bitter water" — water mixed with dust from the tabernacle floor — with a pronounced curse. If guilty, she would suffer physical consequences; if innocent, she would be unharmed and cleared. The ritual served as a divinely guaranteed ordeal that protected both the accused innocent wife and the integrity of the covenant community. The emphasis on God as the judge reflects the limitation of human judicial process where no human witness was available.
